area: Data/research platform, news & event analysis, and structural gaps

departments.name: Data Engineering & Research Platform
departments.mandate: Own the firm's research data estate and computation platform end-to-end: acquire, license, ingest, validate, and serve internal and third-party data with point-in-time (bitemporal) correctness; and provide reproducible research infrastructure (versioned data, code, environments, experiment registry) so that any investment-relevant research artifact can be re-derived, audited, and produced to regulators or internal assurance functions on demand.
responsibilities: Data vendor lifecycle management across the full third-party lifecycle: planning/scouting, trials, due diligence and selection, contract negotiation support, onboarding, ongoing monitoring, and termination (mirrors the 2023 Interagency Third-Party Risk Management guidance lifecycle)

Alternative-data diligence coordination with Compliance: documented, consistently applied written diligence covering data provenance, collection consent, web-scraping/ToS exposure, PII handling, and MNPI risk attestations (SEC April 2022 EXAMS risk alert explicitly criticized ad hoc, inconsistent, undocumented diligence)
Ingestion pipeline engineering with schema/contract enforcement, deduplication, and delivery SLA monitoring
Security master / symbology and entity mastering: identifier crosswalks (ticker/CUSIP/ISIN/FIGI), corporate-action processing, entity resolution for news and alt-data joins

Data quality controls: completeness, timeliness, accuracy and consistency checks; anomaly detection; vendor restatement tracking; quarantine-and-incident process with documented exception handling (BCBS 239 principles adopted by analogy)

Point-in-time correctness: bitemporal storage (event date vs. knowledge/as-of date), PIT vendor snapshots, retention of delisted/dead securities to prevent survivorship bias, restatement-aware fundamentals to prevent look-ahead bias

Research reproducibility infrastructure: dataset versioning, code version control, pinned environments, immutable experiment/backtest registry, end-to-end lineage from raw vendor file to signal to recommendation
Entitlement and access management enforcing license terms, information barriers (MNPI walls), and PII restrictions
Data-spend management: usage metering against vendor licenses, renewal economics, duplicate-feed rationalization
Recordkeeping support: retention and prompt-production capability for research records consistent with Advisers Act Rule 204-2 (five-plus years, first two easily accessible)

workflows.name: Vendor trial and onboarding
workflows.cadence: Per vendor, ad hoc (typically 4-12 weeks per vendor)
workflows.description: Structured evaluation and onboarding of a new data vendor: diligence questionnaire (provenance, consent, MNPI/ToS/PII attestations), sample-data evaluation on the platform, Compliance sign-off, contract execution, entitlement configuration, ingestion spec, production cutover.
workflows.inputs: Vendor questionnaire responses, sample data, license draft, requesting desk's use case, Compliance policy checklist
workflows.outputs: Diligence memo (retained), Compliance approval record, executed contract, entitlement config, ingestion spec, catalog entry

workflows.name: Ingestion and data-quality gate
workflows.description: Automated ingestion with validation gates (schema, completeness, timeliness, statistical anomaly checks); failures quarantine the batch and open an incident rather than propagating to research consumers.
workflows.inputs: Vendor feeds (market, fundamental, alternative), internal trade/position data, DQ rule definitions
workflows.outputs: Validated curated datasets, DQ dashboard/scorecards, quarantine log, incident tickets

workflows.name: Point-in-time snapshotting and restatement processing
workflows.cadence: Daily, plus on vendor restatement events
workflows.description: Capture as-of snapshots of mutable datasets (fundamentals, estimates, index membership); process vendor restatements into bitemporal tables preserving what-was-known-when; audit-log every retroactive change.
workflows.inputs: Raw vendor deliveries, restatement notices, prior snapshots
workflows.outputs: Bitemporal tables, restatement audit log, PIT-correctness test results

workflows.name: Symbology and corporate-action mastering
workflows.description: Maintain the security/entity master; apply corporate actions; resolve identifier changes so historical joins remain correct including for delisted names.
workflows.inputs: Exchange/reference-data feeds, corporate-action feeds, vendor identifier files
workflows.outputs: Security master file, identifier crosswalks, dead-security registry

workflows.name: Experiment and backtest registration
workflows.description: Every material backtest/experiment is registered with code commit hash, dataset versions, parameters, environment spec, and results, producing an immutable, re-runnable record supporting model documentation and effective challenge (SR 11-7 lineage / 2026 revised interagency MRM guidance).
workflows.inputs: Code commit, versioned dataset references, parameters, environment manifest
workflows.outputs: Immutable experiment record, results artifact, lineage graph entry

workflows.name: Vendor ongoing monitoring and re-diligence
workflows.description: Score vendors on delivery reliability, data quality drift, and continued compliance representations; re-run diligence at renewal; document termination and data-destruction on offboarding.
workflows.inputs: SLA metrics, DQ trend data, updated vendor attestations, contract terms
workflows.outputs: Vendor scorecard, renewed compliance attestation, renewal/termination recommendation

workflows.name: Data incident post-mortem
workflows.description: Root-cause analysis of data errors that reached research or production signals, including impact assessment on live strategies and backtests, and remediation tracking.
workflows.inputs: Incident ticket, lineage graph, affected experiment registry entries
workflows.outputs: Root-cause report, affected-research impact assessment, remediation actions

workflows.name: Access and entitlement recertification
workflows.description: Review who can access which datasets against license terms, information barriers, and least-privilege; revoke stale access; evidence retained for audit.
workflows.inputs: Entitlement records, HR joiner/leaver data, license terms, barrier lists from Compliance

controls: Written, consistently applied and documented alternative-data diligence policies and procedures (regulatory expectation: Advisers Act Section 204A; SEC EXAMS April 2022 risk alert deficiencies; App Annie enforcement precedent)

Third-party lifecycle controls modeled on the 2023 Interagency TPRM guidance (planning, due diligence, contract, ongoing monitoring, termination) — banking guidance, adopted as institutional practice by managers
Data quality thresholds with automated gates, quarantine authority, and documented exception/override handling (BCBS 239 principles by analogy)
Bitemporal integrity tests preventing silent retroactive mutation of history; retention of delisted securities
Immutable, versioned experiment registry supporting model documentation and independent validation (SR 11-7 and the April 2026 revised interagency MRM guidance, SR 26-02 / OCC 2026-13)
Entitlement enforcement tied to license terms and information barriers; quarterly recertification
Change management and segregation of duties for pipeline and reference-data changes

Records retention and prompt-production consistent with Advisers Act Rule 204-2 (>=5 years, first 2 easily accessible; electronic records safeguarded against loss/alteration with access limited to authorized personnel)
departments.independence: Shared service (first-line function) serving all investment teams; not independent in the assurance sense. Two carve-outs: (1) vendor-diligence approval requires sign-off from an independent Compliance function; (2) the data-quality function should hold quarantine authority that cannot be overridden by a PM without a documented escalation. Independent validation of models built on the platform sits outside this department (second line).
dependencies: Compliance (MNPI determinations, diligence sign-off, information-barrier definitions)
Legal (contract negotiation, ToS/IP/privacy review of data sources)
IT / Information Security (infrastructure, access control, resilience)
Quant Research and PM desks (requirements, data feedback, error reports)
Trading/Operations (execution, position, and corporate-action data as inputs)
Finance (data budget and vendor spend approval)

handoffs.content: Curated point-in-time datasets, data catalog entries with known-issue flags, lineage metadata, experiment-registry services
New-vendor diligence packets for approval; dataset usage logs and diligence records for regulatory exams
Approved/prohibited vendor determinations, restricted-data flags, MNPI rulings, information-barrier lists
Draft vendor contracts and flagged ToS/privacy/IP issues for review; termination notices requiring data-destruction certification
Versioned input datasets, lineage graphs, and experiment records enabling independent revalidation of research models
Executions, positions, corporate-action confirmations, broker data feeding research datasets
Reproducibility evidence: experiment registry extracts, DQ incident history, entitlement recertification records, diligence memos
Entity-mapped, deduplicated news/filings feeds, archives, and NLP enrichment services

regulatoryBasis: Investment Advisers Act Section 204A and Rule 204A-1: duty to maintain written policies reasonably designed to prevent misuse of MNPI — extended by the SEC Division of Examinations April 2022 risk alert to alternative-data sourcing (regulatory requirement for RIAs)
SEC v. App Annie (Sept 2021, $10M penalty): first alt-data-vendor enforcement; establishes that funds must diligence vendor representations about MNPI and data provenance (enforcement precedent)
Advisers Act Rule 204-2 (17 CFR 275.204-2): books-and-records retention including records relating to recommendations and advice (regulatory requirement for RIAs)

Interagency Guidance on Third-Party Relationships: Risk Management (Fed/OCC/FDIC, June 2023): lifecycle framework — binding expectations for banking organizations only; persuasive institutional practice for fund managers

SR 11-7 (2011) and its April 2026 replacement (revised interagency model risk management guidance, SR 26-02 / OCC Bulletin 2026-13): data quality and documentation underpinning models; applies to banking organizations >$30B assets, explicitly excludes generative/agentic AI, and is non-enforceable guidance — hedge funds adopt it voluntarily as the de facto benchmark
BCBS 239 (2013): risk-data aggregation principles (governance, accuracy, completeness, timeliness, lineage) — bank regulation, adopted by analogy as data-governance good practice
practiceNotes: Point-in-time/bitemporal data is widely accepted institutional practice, not a regulation: practitioner literature estimates survivorship bias inflates hedge-fund index returns ~2-4%/yr and equity backtests on current index constituents by ~1-2%/yr; look-ahead bias from restated fundamentals is regarded as the most dangerous silent bias

Reproducibility tooling (data versioning, experiment tracking, pinned environments) is accepted practice at mature quant firms but implementation depth varies widely; there is no regulatory mandate naming specific tools

Centralized vs. federated data engineering varies by firm: multi-manager platforms centralize market data, symbology, and entitlements while pods may retain proprietary alt-data pipelines; single-strategy quant firms usually fully centralize
Data-spend governance (usage metering, duplicate-feed rationalization) is a growing practice area as alt-data budgets grow; judgment call on where it reports (COO vs. CTO vs. head of research)

departments.name: News & Event Analysis
departments.mandate: Systematically monitor news, regulatory filings, and event flow across firm positions and watchlists; triage items for materiality within defined SLAs; verify source authenticity and credibility before signals reach portfolio managers; maintain catalyst/event calendars; and route potential MNPI or manipulation indicators to Compliance rather than to the investment process.
responsibilities: Real-time monitoring of tiered sources: regulatory filings (8-K, Form 4, 13D/G), regulator portals (FDA, antitrust/competition authorities, court dockets), newswires, company press releases and IR pages, index-provider notices, and (as a low-credibility tier) social media

Materiality triage against positions and watchlists: tiered alerting — Tier 1 instant alerts (8-Ks, M&A announcements, recalls, halts), Tier 2 digest items (IR calendar changes, deck uploads) — per institutional monitoring practice

Source credibility and authenticity verification: cross-confirmation rules before any single-source item is actionable; hoax/fake-press-release detection (SEC has repeatedly charged fake-release manipulation schemes, e.g., Marketwired-distributed false releases in 2015 and the 2021 phony-press-release scheme with >$10M illicit profits)
Catalyst calendar maintenance: earnings dates, FDA PDUFA dates, court rulings, regulatory decision deadlines, index rebalances, macro releases
Event post-mortems measuring detection latency, routing accuracy, and action taken

MNPI hygiene: any item suspected to be non-public routes to Compliance first; no retransmission of unverified market rumors (market-abuse exposure under Exchange Act 10b-5 and, in the EU/UK, MAR Article 12 dissemination provisions)

workflows.name: Continuous monitoring and tiered alerting
workflows.cadence: Market hours minimum; 24x5 with overnight coverage at large multi-strategy firms
workflows.description: Automated feed monitoring with entity mapping to positions/watchlists; tier-1 items page the owning analyst/PM and (as configured) Risk and Compliance; tier-2 items batch into digests.
workflows.inputs: News/filings feeds (entity-mapped by Data Engineering), position and watchlist data, source-tier policy
workflows.outputs: Routed alerts with materiality tier and position context; alert audit log

workflows.name: Materiality triage and escalation
workflows.description: Human/analyst triage of automated alerts: confirm relevance, size against exposure, tag materiality, escalate to PM and Risk; suppress noise with documented rationale.
workflows.inputs: Raw alerts, exposure data from Risk, catalyst calendar
workflows.outputs: Triaged event ticket (materiality tag, owner, deadline), escalation notifications

workflows.name: Source verification protocol
workflows.description: Before an unconfirmed item becomes actionable: verify wire authenticity, confirm via issuer IR page or regulator source, check domain/spoofing indicators; unverified items are flagged block-on-unverified.
workflows.inputs: Suspect item, issuer IR contacts, wire-service verification channels

workflows.name: Daily event briefing
workflows.cadence: Daily pre-open (and post-close summary at some firms)
workflows.description: Digest of overnight events, day-ahead catalysts, and open event tickets distributed to desks and Risk.
workflows.inputs: Overnight alert log, catalyst calendar, desk watchlists

workflows.name: Catalyst calendar review
workflows.description: Reconcile the catalyst calendar against desk expectations and vendor calendars; identify coverage gaps for upcoming binary events.
workflows.inputs: Vendor calendars, desk catalyst hypotheses, prior calendar

workflows.name: Post-event review
workflows.description: Measure detection-to-alert latency, routing correctness, and whether credibility protocol was followed; feed improvements back into tiering rules.
workflows.inputs: Alert audit log, event tickets, market timestamps

controls: Source-tier policy with documented credibility ratings and single-source trading restrictions
Mandatory verification protocol for unconfirmed market-moving items (fake-press-release/hoax risk is an established SEC enforcement pattern)
MNPI escalation procedure: suspected non-public items go to Compliance before any desk distribution (Advisers Act 204A)
Prohibition on retransmitting unverified rumors (market-abuse exposure: Exchange Act 10b-5; EU MAR Art. 12 for EU-touching activity)
Complete audit log of alerts, triage decisions, and distributions retained as records (Advisers Act Rule 204-2)
News-vendor license compliance for internal redistribution and archiving
Escalation-path testing and coverage roster for overnight/holiday gaps
departments.independence: Judgment call, not a regulatory matter — three observed models: (a) standalone centralized news/event desk (large multi-strategy and event-driven firms; consistent triage, 24-hour coverage, economies of scale on feeds); (b) fully embedded in each pod/desk (smaller funds; better context, duplicated spend, inconsistent MNPI hygiene); (c) hybrid — centralized monitoring utility as a shared service with embedded desk-level triage. Recommendation for this harness: hybrid, modeled as a distinct department because it exercises cross-department routing (PM, Risk, Compliance, Data Engineering) well. Quant-heavy firms often treat news purely as a data feed owned by Data Engineering with NLP enrichment, which is a legitimate alternative.
dependencies: Data Engineering & Research Platform (feed infrastructure, entity mapping, deduplication, archives, NLP enrichment)
PM desks (watchlists, catalyst hypotheses, materiality feedback)
Risk Management (exposure data for materiality scoring)

handoffs.content: Triaged, credibility-scored event alerts with position context and materiality tier; daily event digest and catalyst calendar
Immediate escalation of suspected MNPI, manipulation indicators, or unverifiable market-moving items
Material event notifications touching concentrated or leveraged exposures
Entity-mapped news/filings feeds, historical archives, dedup and enrichment services
Watchlists, position changes, and catalyst hypotheses defining monitoring scope
Verified time-critical event notifications on held names (whether this desk can trigger orders directly varies by firm — usually it cannot; PM authorization required)

regulatoryBasis: No regulation mandates a news/event desk — the structure is entirely an operating-model choice (judgment call)
Advisers Act Section 204A: written procedures to prevent MNPI misuse apply to information arriving via news/rumor channels (regulatory requirement)

Exchange Act Section 10(b)/Rule 10b-5 and EU Market Abuse Regulation Art. 12: trading on or spreading false information is market manipulation; SEC enforcement against fake press-release schemes (2015 Marketwired-distributed releases; 2021 phony-release scheme) motivates the verification protocol (regulatory requirement as a boundary condition)
Advisers Act Rule 204-2: alert/triage records relating to recommendations are retainable books and records (regulatory requirement)
practiceNotes: Institutional monitoring practice converges on tiered alerting (instant vs. digest) and routing by role (PM queue, risk, compliance) — supported by vendor/practitioner literature rather than regulation
Event-driven funds treat catalyst tracking as core front-office alpha work (event probability, post-event valuation); at such firms this function is front office, not support
Detection-latency metrics and post-event reviews are a maturing practice; few firms publish benchmarks

AI/LLM-based news summarization is increasingly used for first-pass triage; note the April 2026 revised interagency MRM guidance explicitly excludes generative/agentic AI from its scope, leaving a governance gap firms must fill with internal policy

structuralRecommendations: SPLIT (internal): within Data Engineering & Research Platform, separate 'Data Sourcing & Vendor Management' (compliance-heavy contract/diligence lifecycle) from 'Platform Engineering & Data Quality' (technical pipelines, PIT stores, reproducibility) as two sub-functions under one accountable head — the workflow families, skills, and control counterparties are distinct.

KEEP AS DISTINCT DEPARTMENT (hybrid staffing): News & Event Analysis should be modeled as its own department in the harness (central monitoring utility + embedded desk triage), because it exercises realistic cross-department routing to PM, Risk, Compliance, and Data Engineering; note that fully standalone desks are typical only at multi-manager scale and quant firms often fold news into Data Engineering as a feed.

ADD (in scope): Research/Investment Compliance function (MNPI controls, alt-data diligence sign-off, expert-network chaperoning, restricted/watch lists) — regulatorily unavoidable (Advisers Act 204A, SEC April 2022 risk alert, App Annie); without it, the vendor-onboarding and MNPI-escalation workflows in this area have no counterparty.

ADD (in scope, thin): Model Risk / Independent Validation as a second-line function (effective challenge of research models per SR 11-7 and its April 2026 replacement) — may be merged into a broader Risk department if one exists in the harness, but the independence property must be preserved.

ADD (in scope, thin interface): Execution/Trading desk as a boundary object — research recommendation -> order staging -> execution report closes the research data loop, and execution/position data is a critical research-platform input; full trading operations (algo suites, TCA vendor management, settlement) are OUT of scope for a research-platform harness.
ADD (in scope if not covered elsewhere): Market/Portfolio Risk Management as a consumer of research outputs and provider of exposure data for news materiality scoring.

OUT OF SCOPE (model as external handoff endpoints only): Legal (contract review service in vendor onboarding), Investor Relations (client-facing; no daily research artifacts), Internal Audit (third-line periodic assurance — represent as an audit-evidence export requirement on the platform, not a workflow department, per IIA Three Lines Model), Valuation Committee/Pricing (fund-ops NAV domain per AIMA valuation sound practices; retain one handoff — research inputs for hard-to-value positions), Fund Accounting/Administration, Treasury/Financing, HR/Facilities — none of these consume or produce research artifacts at daily cadence, and modeling them as endpoints keeps the harness realistic without bloat.

SCOPE BOUNDARY (performance): keep backtest and live performance analytics inside the research platform; official GIPS composite construction/reporting is OUT of scope — GIPS is a voluntary, firm-wide, marketing-facing standard and relatively uncommon among hedge funds.
disagreementsAndVariation: Standalone vs. embedded news desk: no industry consensus; varies by AUM, strategy (event-driven treats it as front office; quant treats it as a data feed), and time-zone footprint. This document recommends hybrid but flags it as a judgment call.

SR 11-7 applicability: it is banking-organization guidance (and its April 2026 replacement applies to banks >$30B and is explicitly non-enforceable); hedge funds adopt it voluntarily as benchmark practice. The 2026 revision explicitly excludes generative/agentic AI, so firms using LLM-based research tooling face a guidance vacuum — internal policy must fill it, and practice diverges sharply.
BCBS 239 is bank regulation; asset managers adopt its lineage/quality principles selectively — citing it as 'required' for a fund would be wrong.

Data-quarantine authority: whether the central data team can block data a PM wants to use varies — centralized multi-manager platforms enforce quarantine; pod-autonomy firms often allow pods to self-source, accepting inconsistency.

Depth of alternative-data diligence varies from questionnaire-only to full legal provenance audits; the SEC's criticism targeted inconsistency and lack of documentation rather than mandating a specific depth.
GIPS compliance is widespread among traditional asset managers but uncommon among hedge funds; it is recommended practice, never a legal requirement.

Best-execution reporting regimes diverge: the EU dropped RTS 28 public reporting under the MiFID II review while retaining the Art. 27 best-execution obligation; the US relies on the adviser's fiduciary duty — a harness should not hard-code one jurisdiction's reporting artifact.
Sources on point-in-time bias magnitudes (2-4%/yr hedge fund survivorship, 1-2%/yr equity constituent bias) are practitioner estimates, not regulatory findings; treat as order-of-magnitude.

sourcesConsulted.whatItSupported: SR 11-7 supervisory guidance on model risk management: development, independent validation/effective challenge, governance, documentation
Revised interagency model risk management guidance (SR 26-02) replacing SR 11-7; emphasis on data quality infrastructure underpinning models
Confirmation that the April 17, 2026 revised MRM guidance replaces the 2011 guidance, targets banks >$30B, excludes generative/agentic AI, and is non-enforceable
SEC App Annie enforcement (Sept 2021, $10M): alt-data vendor misrepresentation of MNPI controls; first alt-data vendor action
SEC EXAMS observations on alternative data: deficiencies in memorializing and consistently applying vendor diligence
SEC Division of Examinations April 26, 2022 risk alert (Investment Adviser MNPI Compliance Issues): alt-data policy deficiencies under Section 204A
Same April 2022 risk alert: definition of alternative data and expectation of reasonably designed written P&Ps for alt-data MNPI risk
Advisers Act Rule 204-2 books-and-records text: covered records and 5-year/first-2-years-accessible retention; electronic recordkeeping safeguards
2023 Interagency Third-Party Risk Management guidance: five-stage lifecycle (planning, due diligence/selection, contract, ongoing monitoring, termination)
BCBS 239 principles: governance/infrastructure, accuracy and completeness, timeliness, adaptability; lineage and data-quality expectations (bank regulation, used by analogy)
IIA Three Lines Model (2020): first/second-line management roles vs. third-line internal audit independence — basis for classifying internal audit as out-of-scope assurance endpoint

AIMA Guide to Sound Practices for Hedge Fund Valuation: board responsibility, valuation committee/governance recommendations — basis for placing valuation outside the research platform with a defined handoff
AIMA 2018 valuation sound-practices guide (16 recommendations across governance, transparency, procedures, sources/models) as filed with the SEC
GIPS 2020 for firms: firm-wide voluntary compliance, composite requirements, verification recommended — basis for scoping GIPS reporting out of the research harness
ESMA position on RTS 28: detailed venue/execution-quality reports no longer required under reviewed MiFID II, while Art. 27 best-execution duty remains
ESMA final report on MiFID II order execution policy RTS: written execution policy, annual review, monitoring obligations
Event-driven strategy primer: alpha from corporate catalysts, event probabilities, post-event valuation — front-office framing of event analysis

Institutional monitoring practice (vendor literature, lower authority — labeled as practice not regulation): tiered alerting (Tier 1 instant 8-K/recall vs Tier 2 digest), routing to PM/risk/compliance queues
Scharfman/CAIA hedge fund compliance chapter: expert-network risks, restricted lists, call chaperoning, call limits
Expert-network vetting practices: chaperoning, cooling-off periods, MNPI training expectations
SEC enforcement pattern against fake-news/paid-promotion schemes — motivation for source-verification controls
Fake press-release manipulation cases (incl. 2015 Marketwired-distributed releases; 2021 phony-release scheme >$10M) — verification-protocol rationale
Point-in-time data as the accepted remedy for look-ahead/survivorship bias in backtesting (vendor practice literature)
Practitioner taxonomy of backtest biases; look-ahead bias characterized as the most dangerous/silent (practitioner literature, order-of-magnitude estimates only)
Standard hedge fund front/middle/back-office function inventory used for the structural gap scan (trading, IR, treasury, fund accounting, reconciliation, compliance, technology)
AIMA hedge fund start-up guide: expected functions and service-provider relationships in an institutional-quality manager
